claim: Cohen's kappa and similar metrics account for agreement by pure chance, making inter-rater agreement meaningful by comparing to a random baseline
actionable: Use Cohen's kappa or equivalent metrics instead of raw agreement rates to assess human rater consistency against baseline random agreement
source_title: Stanford CME295 Transformers & LLMs | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8 - LLM Evaluation

claim: Rule-based metrics (METEOR, BLEU, ROUGE) fail at stylistic variation; identical meaning stated differently will score poorly
actionable: Avoid sole reliance on BLEU/ROUGE for evaluation; supplement with human judgment or LLM-as-judge for tasks valuing varied expression
source_title: Stanford CME295 Transformers & LLMs | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8 - LLM Evaluation

claim: LLM-as-judge requires structured output (constrained decoding) to guarantee parseable JSON responses with rationale and score
actionable: Use structured output / constrained decoding (e.g., OpenAI's response_format parameter) when using LLM-as-judge to ensure valid JSON
source_title: Stanford CME295 Transformers & LLMs | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8 - LLM Evaluation

claim: Outputting rationale before score (like chain-of-thought) improves LLM-as-judge quality and allows interpretation of scores
actionable: Structure LLM-as-judge prompts to output reasoning/rationale before final score to improve accuracy and interpretability
source_title: Stanford CME295 Transformers & LLMs | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8 - LLM Evaluation

claim: Position bias: LLM-as-judge preferences depend on response order (A vs B); remedy with position-swapped majority voting
actionable: When comparing two responses, ask 'is A or B better' then 'is B or A better' and use majority voting to mitigate position bias
source_title: Stanford CME295 Transformers & LLMs | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8 - LLM Evaluation

claim: Verbosity bias: LLM-as-judge tends to prefer longer responses over shorter ones regardless of quality
actionable: Explicitly instruct judges to ignore length, show length-independent examples in context, or penalize response length in evaluation
source_title: Stanford CME295 Transformers & LLMs | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8 - LLM Evaluation

claim: Self-enhancement bias: models prefer outputs they generated regardless of quality; use different (preferably larger) models for generation and evaluation
actionable: Use a larger, different model as judge rather than the same model that generated responses to reduce self-enhancement bias
source_title: Stanford CME295 Transformers & LLMs | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8 - LLM Evaluation

claim: Binary scales (pass/fail) are preferable to granular scales for LLM-as-judge; humans also find binary judgments easier and less noisy
actionable: Use binary (pass/fail) evaluation scales instead of 5-point scales to reduce judgment noise and improve agreement with human raters
source_title: Stanford CME295 Transformers & LLMs | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8 - LLM Evaluation

claim: Calibrate LLM-as-judge scores against human ratings via correlation analysis; correlation imperfections show where prompts need refinement
actionable: Collect samples of human ratings, run LLM-as-judge on same samples, compute correlation, and iterate on judge prompt until aligned
source_title: Stanford CME295 Transformers & LLMs | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8 - LLM Evaluation

claim: Use low temperature (0.1-0.2) for evaluation to ensure reproducible scores across runs
actionable: Set temperature to 0.1 or 0.2 when running LLM-as-judge to ensure consistent, reproducible evaluation scores
source_title: Stanford CME295 Transformers & LLMs | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8 - LLM Evaluation

claim: Factuality evaluation requires extracting facts from text, fact-checking each (via RAG/search), and aggregating weighted scores; not a binary task
actionable: For factuality: (1) extract claims as facts, (2) verify each via knowledge sources, (3) weight by importance, (4) aggregate scores
source_title: Stanford CME295 Transformers & LLMs | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8 - LLM Evaluation

claim: Tool prediction errors in agents include: missing tool use, tool hallucination, wrong tool selection, and incorrect arguments
actionable: When debugging agent failures, systematically check: tool selection accuracy, hallucinated functions, tool API clarity, and argument correctness
source_title: Stanford CME295 Transformers & LLMs | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8 - LLM Evaluation

claim: Tool hallucination (calling non-existent functions) often stems from unclear API descriptions or weak models; improve function names, arguments, and docstrings
actionable: When models hallucinate functions, first improve API naming, arguments, and descriptions; only upgrade model if this doesn't help
source_title: Stanford CME295 Transformers & LLMs | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8 - LLM Evaluation

claim: Tool outputs must always return meaningful structured data (even empty JSON > no response) so agents can continue reasoning
actionable: Ensure tool implementations always return structured output; return empty JSON if no results rather than null/error
source_title: Stanford CME295 Transformers & LLMs | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8 - LLM Evaluation

claim: MMLU tests knowledge across 60+ diverse domains; uses 4-choice multiple-choice format with hardcoded answer extraction
actionable: Use MMLU for knowledge benchmark; extract predicted letter to compare against hardcoded answers and avoid LLM-as-judge variability
source_title: Stanford CME295 Transformers & LLMs | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8 - LLM Evaluation

claim: AIME (American Invitational Mathematics Exam) requires reasoning steps before a 3-digit numerical answer; good for evaluating math reasoning
actionable: Use AIME benchmark for mathematical reasoning evaluation; focus on models' ability to decompose problems, not just recall
source_title: Stanford CME295 Transformers & LLMs | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8 - LLM Evaluation

claim: PIQA (Physical Interaction Question Answering) tests common-sense reasoning about everyday physics with 2-choice format and 20k examples
actionable: Use PIQA to evaluate common-sense reasoning; its 2-choice format is simpler than 4-choice and grounds reasoning in real-world physics
source_title: Stanford CME295 Transformers & LLMs | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8 - LLM Evaluation

claim: SWE-bench tests code fixing using GitHub issues with pre-computed tests; passing tests indicates successful bug fix
actionable: Use SWE-bench to evaluate coding ability; run model's patch against test suite and measure pass rate for objective assessment
source_title: Stanford CME295 Transformers & LLMs | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8 - LLM Evaluation

claim: HarmBench tests safety across standard, copyright, contextual, and multimodal categories; uses classifiers for open-ended harmful behavior detection
actionable: Use HarmBench to measure safety; understand its 4 categories differ from your org's safety policy, so calibrate expectations
source_title: Stanford CME295 Transformers & LLMs | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8 - LLM Evaluation

claim: Tau-Bench (Tool Agent Users Benchmark) uses separate LLM to simulate user interactions in agentic workflows for realistic evaluation
actionable: For agent evaluation, use Tau-Bench or similar multi-turn simulated environments; avoid single-turn evaluation for agents
source_title: Stanford CME295 Transformers & LLMs | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8 - LLM Evaluation

claim: Pass@all-K (probability all K attempts succeed) is more relevant than pass@K for agent reliability; matters when consistency is critical
actionable: For agents in high-stakes domains, measure pass@all-K not pass@K to ensure consistent reliability across multiple attempts
source_title: Stanford CME295 Transformers & LLMs | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8 - LLM Evaluation

claim: Benchmarks characterize model profile (strengths/weaknesses), not absolute quality; different models excel at different tasks and prices
actionable: Evaluate models on Pareto frontier: plot performance vs. cost/latency and select model fitting your constraints, not absolute performance
source_title: Stanford CME295 Transformers & LLMs | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8 - LLM Evaluation

claim: Data contamination risk: ensure models haven't seen benchmark answers; use hash values or blocklists to verify test set separation
actionable: Verify no data contamination: check model training cutoff vs benchmark dates, use hash-based blocklists to prevent benchmark access
source_title: Stanford CME295 Transformers & LLMs | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8 - LLM Evaluation
